content:
# Acts Chapter 20 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 250-251 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_020_page_001.jpg, ACT_020_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And after the uproar ceased, Paul, having sent-for the disciples and having exhorted [them], having embraced [them], departed to journey into Macedonia.

**2** And having passed-through those parts and having exhorted them with much speech, he came into Greece.

**3** And having spent three months, a plot having been made against him by the Jews [as he was] about to set-sail into Syria, [a] decision came-about to return through Macedonia.

**4** And there accompanied him Sopater [son] of Pyrrhus, a Berean; and of [the] Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus; and Gaius of Derbe, and Timothy; and of [the] Asians, Tychicus and Trophimus.

**5** And these, having gone-ahead, were waiting for us in Troas.

**6** And we sailed-out from Philippi after the days of Unleavened-Bread, and came to them in Troas within five days, where we stayed seven days.

**7** And on the first [day] of the week, we having been gathered-together to break bread, Paul was discoursing to them, being-about to depart on the next-day, and he was extending the speech until midnight.

**8** And there were sufficient lamps in the upper-room where we were gathered-together.

**9** And a certain young-man named Eutychus, sitting upon the window, being borne-down by a deep sleep, while Paul discoursed yet-longer, having been borne-down by the sleep, fell from the third-story down and was taken-up dead.

**10** And Paul, having gone-down, fell-upon him and having embraced [him] said, "Do not be troubled, for his life is in him."

**11** And having gone-up and having broken the bread and having tasted, and having conversed for a considerable-time until dawn, thus he departed.

**12** And they brought the boy alive and were comforted not moderately.

**13** And we, having gone-ahead to the ship, set-sail for Assos, from-there being-about to take-up Paul; for thus he had arranged, being-about himself to go-on-foot.

**14** And when he met us at Assos, having taken him up, we came to Mitylene.

**15** And having sailed-from-there, on the following [day] we arrived opposite Chios; and on the next [day] we crossed-over to Samos; and on the following [day] we came to Miletus.

**16** For Paul had decided to sail-past Ephesus, so-that it might not happen to him to spend-time in Asia; for he was hastening, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost.

**17** And from Miletus, having sent to Ephesus, he summoned the elders of the assembly.

**18** And when they came to him, he said to them: "You yourselves know, from the first day on which I set-foot in Asia, how I was with you the whole time,

**19** serving the Lord with all humility and tears and trials, the [ones] having come-upon me by the plots of the Jews;

**20** how I held-back nothing of the [things] profitable [so as] not to declare to you and to teach you publicly and from house-to-house,

**21** testifying both to Jews and to Greeks repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ.

**22** And now, behold, I, having been bound in the Spirit, am journeying to Jerusalem, the [things] in it [that will] meet me not knowing,

**23** except that the Holy Spirit in every city testifies to me, saying that bonds and afflictions await me.

**24** But I make of no account [my] life [as] precious to myself, so-that I might finish my course and the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus, to testify the good-news of the grace of God.

**25** And now, behold, I know that no-longer will you see my face, all of you among whom I went-about proclaiming the kingdom.

**26** Therefore I testify to you on this very day that I [am] clean from the blood of all,

**27** for I did not shrink-back from declaring to you all the counsel of God.

**28** Take-heed to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit placed you [as] overseers, to shepherd the assembly of God, which he obtained through the blood of his own [Son].

**29** I know that after my departure grievous wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock;

**30** and from among your own selves will arise men speaking distorted-things, to draw-away the disciples after themselves.

**31** Therefore be-watchful, remembering that for three years, night and day, I did not cease admonishing each one with tears.

**32** And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, the [one] being able to build-up and to give the inheritance among all the [ones] having been sanctified.

**33** Silver or gold or clothing of no-one did I covet.

**34** You yourselves know that these hands ministered to my needs and to the [ones] being with me.

**35** In all-things I showed you that, thus laboring, it is necessary to support the [ones] being weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said, 'It is more blessed to give than to receive.'"

**36** And having said these-things, having knelt with all of them, he prayed.

**37** And considerable weeping of all came-about, and having fallen-upon the neck of Paul, they were kissing him,

**38** grieving most-of-all over the word which he had spoken, that they are-about no-longer to behold his face. And they were escorting him to the ship.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 4 -- Sopater's patronymic:** Sinaiticus reads ΠΥΡΡΟΥ (Pyrrhus), identifying Sopater as "son of Pyrrhus." Some later manuscripts omit this patronymic. The Sinaiticus reading is supported by Alexandrian witnesses.

2. **Verse 4 -- Gaius of Derbe:** Sinaiticus reads ΔΕΡΒΑΙΟΣ (Derbean). The Byzantine text tradition sometimes reads "Doberian" (from Doberus in Macedonia). The Sinaiticus reading, associating Gaius with Derbe in Lycaonia, is the better attested.

3. **Verse 15 -- Itinerary at Samos:** Sinaiticus appears to omit the phrase "and having remained in Trogyllium" (KAI MEINANTEC EN ΤΡΩΓΥΛΛΙΩ) which appears in the Western text (Codex D) and some Byzantine manuscripts. This is a well-known Western addition to the travel itinerary.

4. **Verse 21 -- "Lord Jesus Christ" vs. "Lord Jesus":** Sinaiticus reads ΚΥΡΙΟΝ ΙΗΣΟΥΝ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ (Lord Jesus Christ) with the full title. Some manuscripts read simply "Lord Jesus" without "Christ."

5. **Verse 24 -- Textual complexity:** This verse shows significant variation across manuscripts. Sinaiticus reads the course of the statement somewhat differently from the Textus Receptus. The phrase "with joy" (ΜΕΤΑ ΧΑΡΑΣ) found in some later manuscripts does not appear in the Sinaiticus text. The Sinaiticus reading is more austere: Paul makes no account of his life as precious, in order to finish his course and ministry.

6. **Verse 25 -- "the kingdom":** Sinaiticus reads simply ΤΗΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑΝ ("the kingdom") without the addition "of God" (ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ) found in some later witnesses. This shorter reading is characteristic of the Alexandrian text-type.

7. **Verse 28 -- "assembly of God":** Sinaiticus reads ΘΕΟΥ ("of God") rather than ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ("of the Lord"). This is a theologically significant variant: "the assembly of God, which he obtained through the blood of his own" -- the reading ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ creates a direct statement that God obtained the church through blood, which later scribes may have found theologically difficult, leading to the variant "of the Lord." The Sinaiticus reading is supported by P74, Vaticanus, and other early witnesses.

8. **Verse 28 -- "through the blood of his own [Son]":** The Greek ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΑΙΜΑΤΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΙΔΙΟΥ is ambiguous. It could mean "through his own blood" (referring directly to God's blood, a startling theological claim) or "through the blood of his Own [One]" -- i.e., his own Son. The latter reading, treating ΙΔΙΟΥ as a substantive ("his Own"), is likely intended and avoids the theological difficulty. I have rendered it "his own [Son]" with [Son] in brackets to indicate the implied referent.

9. **Verse 35 -- Agraphon of Jesus:** The saying "It is more blessed to give than to receive" is not found in any canonical Gospel. This is the most famous agraphon (unwritten saying) of Jesus preserved outside the Gospels. Paul attributes it directly to the Lord Jesus.

10. **Verse 28 -- "overseers" (ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΟΥΣ):** I have translated this literally as "overseers" rather than the later ecclesiastical term "bishops." In this earliest usage, the term is applied to those also called "elders" (ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΕΡΟΥΣ, v. 17), indicating the terms were functionally equivalent in the apostolic period.

-- EMET
